title: Alberti, Palazzo Rucellai
topic_path: Sculpture and architecture in central Italy

assessment_items.question_content:
**Alberti designed the Palazzo Rucellai so that it would follow Renaissance values of architecture. It was the first private residence that employed [[☃ dropdown 1]] in its design.**


![Renaissance Palazzo Rucellai in Florence, with symmetrical stone façade, arched windows, and classical detailing.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0a2e8592fbfa026b2467ff51c16f4bed.jpg)
hints:
**Alberti designed the Palazzo Rucellai so that it would follow Renaissance values of architecture. It was the first private residence that employed Humanism in its design.**

**[Read this essay to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/florence-1/a/alberti-palazzo-rucellai)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Each floor of the Palazzo Rucellai served a different purpose. Please match the floors to their purpose.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
**The Palazzo Rucellai had four floors: the first was where the family conducted their business; the second floor, or piano nobile, was where they received guests; the third floor contained the family’s private apartments; and a hidden fourth floor, which had few windows and is invisible from the street, was where the servants lived.** 

**[Read this essay to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/florence-1/a/alberti-palazzo-rucellai)**

left: First floor ![Exterior of the Sistine Chapel in Vatican City, showing a plain rectangular stone façade with small windows and a central doorway.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/75879a236dcd9e52654a0a76719c45ba.jpg)
Second floor ![Renaissance-style building façade with arched windows framed by decorative stonework and detailed molding.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cbd613857d516d9ee6118a18d91b4168.jpg)
Third Floor ![Upper section of a Renaissance building with arched windows, stone detailing, and decorative cornice molding.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ef1957fd8d765fd43c2ebbff81470511.jpg)
Fourth floor (invisible) ![Close-up of a Renaissance building cornice with decorative stone brackets and molding.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c3ab5e6d6434290c828910024dd3be39.jpg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Palazzo Rucellai is similar to which Ancient Roman structure?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**The Palazzo Rucellai is very similar to the Colosseum.  The great Roman amphitheater is also divided into bays. More importantly, it uses architectural features for decorative purposes rather than structural support; like the engaged columns on the Colosseum, the pilasters on the façade of the Rucellai do nothing to actually hold the building up.**

**[Read this essay to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/florence-1/a/alberti-palazzo-rucellai)**

choices.content: Colosseum  ![The Colosseum in Rome illuminated at night, with its arches glowing against a deep blue sky.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/01dbdffe98e6bdd994fe9153af8c778e.jpg)
Pantheon ![Aerial view of the Pantheon in Rome, featuring its large domed roof with central oculus and portico with columns.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/556e937809179763211593c8f9c70c62.jpg)
Arch of Titus ![Arch of Titus in Rome, a triumphal arch with a single large archway and carved relief details.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0664a0da108e045666c28efed93a3cab.jpg)
Hadrian's Villa ![Reflecting pool at Hadrian’s Villa in Tivoli, lined with statues and leading to a domed Roman structure.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/71dc502414a78ce72a60a6620187db9b.jpg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**In order for Renaissance buildings to be truly Humanist, they must look to medieval buildings for inspiration.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**In order for Renaissance buildings to be truly Humanist, they must look to classical buildings for inspiration.**

**[Read this essay to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-1400-1500-renaissance-in-italy-and-the-north/florence-1/a/alberti-palazzo-rucellai)**
